CBC Navigation

Federal budget tabled in House of Commons

By Staff Reporter, CBC News - Posted: May 11, 2026 3:30 PM EDT

The federal government tabled its 2026 budget on Monday afternoon in the House of Commons, outlining $48 billion in new spending across housing, clean energy, and health care over the next five years. The budget is the first since the most recent election and includes measures the government says will support middle-class families facing affordability pressures across the country.

Among the headline items: a new federal housing accelerator that the government says will help municipalities approve construction of 250,000 new homes by 2031, an expansion of the child care program, and a refundable tax credit for retrofits aimed at reducing household energy use. Finance officials emphasized that core programs would not be cut, despite an ongoing focus on fiscal restraint.

Opposition parties were sharply critical. The Conservative finance critic called the budget "a missed opportunity to control spending," while the NDP leader said the housing measures did not go nearly far enough to address what he called "a generational crisis." Independent economists were similarly divided, with several noting that the deficit projection exceeded the figure outlined during the spring fiscal update.

Bond markets reacted modestly to the announcement. The Canadian dollar traded slightly lower against the U.S. dollar following the speech, while yields on five-year government bonds rose two basis points. Analysts at major Canadian banks said they were continuing to digest the details, with most flagging the long-term spending trajectory as a key question for credit rating agencies.

The budget bill is expected to be debated over the coming weeks. The government has not yet indicated whether it intends to seek a confidence vote on the bill itself, though parliamentary observers say a vote of some form is likely before the summer recess.

Copyright CBC News.
